edges.rel: is cursed to be born as
exchanged youth for old age with

beats.title: The Matsya Purana: Origin and King Manu's Boon
beats.summary: The chapter begins with a mangala verse praising Shiva, then introduces the Matsya Purana. Suta is asked by sages in Naimisha Forest to narrate the Purana. The story of King Manu is told: he performed severe penance, received a boon from Brahma to protect all beings during the deluge, and later found a small fish (shaphari) that grew rapidly, eventually revealing itself as Vishnu's Matsya avatar.

beats.title: The Matsya Avatar and the Great Dissolution
beats.summary: The chapter describes the cosmic dissolution (pralaya) at the end of a yuga, as narrated by the Matsya avatar to King Manu. It details the sequence of disasters: a 100-year drought, seven scorching suns, fire from various sources (Aurva, poison from Shesha, Shiva's third eye), followed by seven deluge clouds and the flooding of the oceans, leading to a single great ocean. Manu is instructed to build a boat, gather seeds and living beings, and tie it to Matsya's horn for safety. After the dissolution, Manu will become Prajapati and ruler of the next Manvantara.

beats.title: The Cosmic Creation and the Matsya Avatar
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the cosmic creation process. It begins with the sage Mārkaṇḍeya and others asking about creation and dissolution. The narrative recounts how Lord Viṣṇu, in the form of Matsya (the fish), appears to King Manu during the great deluge, instructs him to build a boat, and preserves the seeds of creation. The chapter then describes the emergence of the cosmic egg (Hiraṇyagarbha) from the primordial waters, the manifestation of Brahmā, and the formation of the universe.

beats.title: Creation and the Emergence of Beings
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation process from the primordial egg, the emergence of Brahma as the four-faced creator, the origin of the Vedas and Puranas, the birth of mind-born sons (Marici, Atri, etc.), and the evolution of the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) leading to the manifestation of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. It also details the emergence of Mahat, Ahamkara, and the senses.

beats.title: Creation of the Elements and Brahma's Incestuous Desire
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the creation of the universe through the evolution of the five gross elements (tanmatras) from mind (manas), and the subsequent creation of Brahma's daughter Savitri (also called Satarupa, Gayatri, Sarasvati). Brahma becomes infatuated with her, leading to the emergence of his five faces as he tries to gaze upon her while she circumambulates him. The chapter ends with Brahma marrying Satarupa.

beats.title: The Creation and the Curse of Kamadeva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the divine creation and the relationship between Brahma and his daughter Sarasvati (also called Savitri or Gayatri). It explains that the initial creation is beyond mortal comprehension, governed by rajas guna. Brahma, overcome by desire, approached his daughter, leading to a curse from Brahma on Kamadeva. Kamadeva pleads for a reprieve, and Brahma prophesies that Kamadeva will be reborn as Pradyumna in the Yadava lineage during the Vaivasvata Manvantara, and later as a king in the Bharata dynasty.

beats.title: Creation Lineage and the Story of Kama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the creation lineage from Svayambhuva Manu through his descendants, including Dhruva, and the births of various kings and sages. It also recounts the story of Kama (Cupid) being burned by Rudra and his subsequent rebirth, and describes the creation of the world by Vamadeva (a form of Shiva).

beats.title: The Lineage of Daksha and the Creation of Beings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the lineage of Prajapati Daksha, his creation of various beings, and the story of his sons who were misled by Narada. It details the births of Prithu, the Pracetas, and Daksha's daughters who became the mothers of gods, demons, and other beings.

beats.title: Daksha's Daughters and the Progeny of the Gods
beats.summary: Narada instructs the sons of Daksha to follow the path of their elder brother, but they refuse and suffer. Daksha then begets sixty daughters from Vairini, distributing them to various beings: ten to Dharma, thirteen to Kashyapa, twenty-seven to Soma, four to Arishtanemi, two to Bhrugu's son, two to Krshashva, and two to Angiras. The offspring of these unions are described, including the Vasus, Rudras, and others.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 10)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the genealogy of the demons (asuras) born from Kaśyapa's wives, primarily Diti and Danu. It lists the twelve Ādityas, the descendants of Hiraṇyakaśipu and Hiraṇyākṣa, and the numerous sons of Danu, including Vipracitti and others. The chapter also mentions the daughters of Danu's sons and the Pauloma and Kālakeya demons.

beats.title: Genealogy of Beings from Kaśyapa's Wives
beats.summary: This chapter continues the genealogy of beings born from Kaśyapa and his wives, listing descendants of Pauloma, Mārīca, Vipracitti, Tāmrā, Vinatā, Surasā, Kadrū, and others, including various demons, birds, snakes, and other creatures. It also mentions the birth of the Maruts from Diti.

beats.title: The Madanadvadashi Vrata: Diti's Vow for Sons
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Madanadvadashi vrata (the twelfth-day vow of Kama) as narrated by Suta to the sages. It recounts how Diti, mother of the Daityas, performed severe penance to obtain sons after losing her offspring in the devasura war. The sage Vasistha and others taught her this vow, which involves worship of Kama (desire) and Vishnu, culminating in the birth of forty-nine Maruts. The chapter details the ritual procedure, including the preparation of a pot, offerings, and the mantra for fulfillment.

beats.title: The Madanadvadashi Vrata and the Birth of Indra's Enemy
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the Madanadvadashi vrata (a vow dedicated to Kamadeva) and its procedure, including offerings, homa, and gifts to brahmins. It then narrates the story of Diti's desire for a son to kill Indra, her husband Kashyapa's promise, the performance of a putreshti sacrifice by Apastamba, and the conception of a child. Kashyapa gives Diti detailed instructions for the care of the pregnancy, emphasizing purity, avoidance of certain actions, and devotion to her husband.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts and Prithu's Appointments
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the story of Diti's pregnancy and the birth of the Maruts. After being cursed by Indra, Diti performs a vow to bear a son who would kill Indra. Indra, seeking a loophole, serves Diti and finds her asleep with her feet unclean, allowing him to enter her womb and cut the fetus into seven pieces, which then become forty-nine Maruts. Indra names them Maruts and grants them a share in sacrifices. The chapter also describes King Prithu's establishment of various deities as lords over different domains.

beats.title: Appointment of Lords and Description of Manvantaras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the appointment of various beings as lords over different classes of creatures by Brahma (Pitamaha), including Vasuki as lord of serpents, Takshaka as lord of snakes, Airavata as lord of elephants, Garuda as lord of birds, Ucchaihshravas as lord of horses, the lion as lord of beasts, the bull as lord of cattle, and Plaksha as lord of trees. It then lists the kings of the directions (Dikpalas) appointed by Brahma: Sudharman for the east, Shankhapada for the south, Ketuman for the west, and Hiranyaroman for the north. The chapter transitions to a dialogue where Manu asks Janardana (Vishnu) about the past Manvantaras (ages of Manu). Janardana recounts the first Manvantara of Svayambhuva Manu, listing the seven sages (Marichi, etc.), the Yama gods, and the ten sons of Svayambhuva. Then the second Manvantara of Svarocisha Manu is described, with the Tusita gods, seven sages (Datta, Nishcyavana, etc.), and ten sons. The third Manvantara of Auttami Manu is mentioned, with the Bhavana gods, seven sages (Kaukurundi, Dalbhya, etc.), and ten sons. Finally, the fourth Manvantara of Tamasa Manu is introduced, with the Sadhya gods, seven sages (Kavi, Prithu, etc.), and ten sons.

beats.title: The Manvantaras and the Cycle of Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the Manvantaras (ages of Manu), listing the seven sages, gods, and sons for each Manu, including the past and future Manus. It also discusses the cyclical nature of creation and dissolution, and the ultimate merging with Vishnu.

beats.title: The Story of Prithu and the Milking of the Earth
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Vena, his wickedness, his death at the hands of sages, and the birth of Prithu from his body. Prithu becomes a righteous king and milks the earth (in the form of a cow) to provide sustenance for all beings. Various groups (sages, gods, demons, etc.) also milk the earth for their respective needs.

beats.title: The Solar Dynasty and the Story of Samjna and Chhaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the lineage of the Sun (Aditya) dynasty, focusing on Vivasvan (the Sun god) and his wives, particularly the story of Samjna and her shadow Chhaya. It details the birth of Yama, Yamuna, Manu, and other children, and the conflict arising from Chhaya's partiality and curse on Yama.

beats.title: Yama's Penance and the Origin of the Ashvins
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Yama's penance at Gokarna Tirtha to attain the position of Lokapala and ruler of the Pitrs, and the subsequent events involving Surya, his wife Samjna, and the craftsman Tvashtr. It explains the origin of the Ashvins, the creation of divine weapons from Surya's tejas, and the prohibition of depicting Surya's feet in images.

beats.title: The Transformation of King Ila
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the story of King Ila, son of Vaivasvata Manu, who accidentally enters the sacred Śaravaṇa forest where a boon from Śiva and Pārvatī causes any male being to become female. Ila is transformed into a woman named Ilā. While wandering in the forest, she is seen by Budha (the planet Mercury), who becomes enamored and approaches her in disguise as a brahmin.

beats.title: The Story of Ila and the Origin of the Lunar Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Ila (also known as Sudyumna) who, due to a curse from Parvati, alternates between male and female forms. While in female form as Ilā, she marries Budha and gives birth to Purūravas. The chapter also describes the lineage of Ila's sons and the origin of the Ilāvṛta region.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Ikshvaku Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapter 12, verses 19-43) presents a genealogical list of the Ikshvaku dynasty, tracing the lineage from Ikshvaku through various kings including Mandhata, Harishchandra, Sagara, and Bhagiratha. It includes details of births, marriages, and notable events such as the story of Sagara's two wives and the birth of his 60,000 sons.

beats.title: Ikshvaku Dynasty and Pitr Vamsha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Ch. 12-13) details the lineage of the Ikshvaku dynasty, including kings such as Bhagiratha, Dilipa, Raghu, and Rama, and then transitions to a discussion of the Pitris (ancestors), their classifications, and the story of Menā's daughters, including Umā (Parvati).

beats.title: Sati's Self-Immolation and the Revelation of Her Forms
beats.summary: Sati, daughter of Daksha and wife of Shiva, immolates herself in protest when Daksha insults Shiva by excluding him from a sacrifice. She curses Daksha to be born as a mortal and lose his sacrifice. Shiva then appears, and Daksha propitiates her. Sati reveals her forms and names at various sacred sites, and instructs Daksha to perform penance to regain his status.

beats.title: The 108 Names and Abodes of the Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter lists 108 names and abodes of the Goddess (Devi) as described in the Matsya Purana. It enumerates various forms of the Goddess residing in different sacred places, rivers, mountains, and cities, along with the benefits of reciting or hearing these names.

beats.title: The Origin of Amavasya and the Birth of Satyavati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the origin of the Amavasya (new moon) tithi, the story of the river Acchoda and her penance, and the prophecy of her future birth as Satyavati, who will bear Vyasa. It also mentions the lineage of the Pauravas and the birth of Vicitravirya and Citrangada.

beats.title: The Realms of Ancestors and the Boon to Pīvarī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes various celestial realms and the lineages of sages and deities, focusing on the Pitṛloka (world of ancestors) and the births of several important figures through divine boons. It narrates the story of Pīvarī, a yoginī who receives a boon from Lord Hari to become the wife of Śuka (Vyāsa's son) and later gives birth to sons including Kṛṣṇa, Gaura, and Śambhu. The chapter also lists various classes of Pitṛs (ancestors) and their abodes, emphasizing the importance of śrāddha rituals.

beats.title: The Glory of Pitrs and Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the various classes of Pitrs (ancestors), their origins, abodes, and the proper conduct of śrāddha rituals to honor them. It details the names and characteristics of different Pitr groups, such as Susvadhā, Ājyapā, and Somapā, and their relationships with sages and kings. The chapter also prescribes offerings and items that are pleasing or displeasing to the Pitrs, emphasizing the importance of śrāddha over even deva worship.

beats.title: Manu's Inquiry on Śrāddha
beats.summary: Manu asks Keśava about the proper times, types, and rules for performing śrāddha rituals to honor the ancestors. Keśava explains the three types of śrāddha (nitya, naimittika, kāmya), describes the qualifications of Brāhmaṇas who should be fed, lists those who should be avoided, and gives detailed instructions for the ritual procedure.

beats.title: Śrāddha Ritual Instructions
beats.summary: This chapter provides detailed instructions for performing a Śrāddha ritual, including the preparation of offerings, the arrangement of sacred items, the seating of Brahmins, the offering of piṇḍas (rice balls) to ancestors, and the proper recitation of mantras. It emphasizes the importance of following the prescribed procedures and avoiding anger while remembering Lord Nārāyaṇa.

beats.title: The Procedure of Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana details the proper procedures for performing Śrāddha (rituals for ancestors), including auspicious timings, offerings, and the importance of following the prescribed rites to avoid negative consequences. It also lists specific days and astrological conditions that yield eternal merit.

beats.title: Ritual Instructions for Vaiśvadeva and Pitṛ Offerings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana provides detailed instructions for performing the Vaiśvadeva and Pitṛ rituals, including the selection of brahmins, preparation of vessels, offerings of food and meat, and the specific mantras and procedures to satisfy the ancestors (pitṛs) and the Viśve Devāḥ. It emphasizes the use of silver vessels for pitṛ offerings and lists the duration of satisfaction from various types of meat.

beats.title: Śrāddha Vidhi: Ritual for Ancestors
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapter 17, verses 36-60) provides detailed instructions for performing the Śrāddha ritual, including offerings to ancestors (pitṛs), recitations of Purāṇas and Vedic hymns, distribution of food, and the proper procedures for making offerings with water, sesame seeds, and kuśa grass. It emphasizes the importance of honoring the pitṛs and ensuring their satisfaction for the well-being of the family.

beats.title: Śrāddha and Āśauca Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana details the rituals for śrāddha (funeral rites) and āśauca (period of impurity) after death. It describes the general śrāddha (sādhāraṇa), the auspicious śrāddha (ābhyudayika) for celebrations, and the ekoddiṣṭa śrāddha for a specific deceased person. It also specifies the duration of impurity for different varṇas and the procedures for offering piṇḍa (rice balls) and other rites.

beats.title: Rituals for the Departed: Sapiṇḍīkaraṇa and Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Ch. 18-19) details the rituals of sapiṇḍīkaraṇa (the ceremony integrating the deceased into the ancestral lineage) and the rules for offering śrāddha (funeral rites). It explains the process of offering piṇḍas (rice balls), the role of the pretas (departed spirits), and how offerings reach the ancestors in various forms of existence. The chapter also discusses the importance of nāma (name) and gotra (lineage) in ensuring the offerings reach the intended recipients.

beats.title: The Seven Sons of Kaushika and Their Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Ch. 19-20) narrates the story of the seven sons of the sage Kaushika, who, due to a sinful act of killing a cow and disguising it as a śrāddha offering, undergo a series of rebirths as hunters, deer, and cakravāka birds, eventually attaining liberation through the grace of the pitṛs and their own yogic practice.

beats.title: Brahmadatta and the Insect Lovers
beats.summary: King Brahmadatta, who understands the language of all creatures, observes a pair of insects in his garden. The male insect apologizes to the female for a past infidelity, and she forgives him. Brahmadatta marvels at this. The chapter also introduces seven yogis, three of whom fell from yoga and were reborn as fish, then as humans: one became Brahmadatta, two became his ministers. Brahmadatta's wife is Samnati, daughter of Devala, formerly Kapila. The chapter ends with questions about how Brahmadatta gained his ability and about a family of cakravaka birds.

beats.title: The Story of King Anagha and Brahmadatta
beats.summary: King Anagha of Panchala, desiring a son, worships Vishnu and obtains Brahmadatta, a son with the ability to understand all creatures' languages. Brahmadatta laughs at a pair of ants, and his wife suspects he is mocking her. To prove his ability, he consults an old Brahmin who reveals the ants' conversation. The story illustrates the power of divine grace and the importance of understanding all beings.

beats.title: The Glory of Śrāddha and Sacred Tīrthas
beats.summary: The chapter describes the aftermath of a story about King Brahmadatta and his ministers who regain memory of past lives through the power of śrāddha (ancestral rites). It then shifts to a discourse on the importance of śrāddha, specifying auspicious times, tīrthas (sacred sites), and the eternal benefits of performing rites at places like Gayā, Vārāṇasī, and Prayāga.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sacred Tīrthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana enumerates numerous sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) and rivers, emphasizing their spiritual significance for performing śrāddha rituals and attaining liberation. It highlights the presence of deities like Keśava, Nārasiṃha, and Varāha at these sites, and extols the merits of visiting them.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sacred Tīrthas for Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana enumerates numerous sacred tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) and rivers, emphasizing their efficacy for performing śrāddha (ancestral rites) and other rituals. It highlights that offerings made at these sites yield infinite merit and that even remembrance or sight of them destroys sins. The text lists specific tīrthas such as Mahābodhi, Nāgatīrtha, Avantikā, Veṇā, Godāvarī, Kāverī, and many others, along with their associated deities and benefits.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sacred Tīrthas for Śrāddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana enumerates numerous sacred tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) where performing śrāddha (ancestral rites) yields infinite merit. It emphasizes that the true tīrtha is truth, compassion, and sense control, and that śrāddha done at these places multiplies the fruit.

beats.title: The Glory of Śrāddha and the Origin of Soma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana discusses the rules and significance of performing śrāddha (ancestral rites) at holy places (tīrthas), emphasizing the importance of the kutapa time (midday) and the eight auspicious items. It also narrates the origin of Soma, the lord of the Pitṛs (ancestors), born from the sage Atri's tapas and Brahmā's creation.

beats.title: The Origin and Exploits of Soma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the origin and rise of Soma (the Moon god), his marriage to the daughters of Daksha, his performance of a Rajasuya sacrifice with Vishnu's help, and his subsequent abduction of Tara, the wife of Brihaspati, leading to conflict.

beats.title: The War over Tara and the Birth of Budha
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the war between Soma (the Moon) and Shiva over Tara, the wife of Brihaspati. Soma abducts Tara, and despite pleas from gods, refuses to return her. Shiva, enraged, leads an army against Soma. The battle is fierce, with both sides using divine weapons. Brahma intervenes, stops the war, and convinces Soma to return Tara. Later, Tara gives birth to Budha, who is claimed by both Soma and Brihaspati. Tara eventually reveals Soma as the father, and Soma accepts Budha. Budha later marries Ila and fathers a son.

beats.title: The Story of King Pururavas and Urvashi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the story of King Pururavas, his encounter with the celestial nymph Urvashi, and the consequences of his actions. It describes his birth, his rule, his meeting with Dharma, Artha, and Kama, and the subsequent curse leading to his transformation into a piśāca. The chapter also includes the birth of his sons and the intervention of Bharata.

beats.title: The Lineage of Raji and Yayati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the lineage of King Raji, his sons, and the subsequent conflict with Indra. Raji, through penance, pleased Vishnu and gained invincibility. During a war between devas and asuras, Raji sided with the devas on condition of becoming their lord. After victory, Raji gave the kingdom to Indra and retired to penance. Later, Raji's sons usurped Indra's kingdom. Indra, with Brihaspati's help, used deception to cause the sons to abandon Vedic dharma, after which Indra killed them. The chapter then introduces Nahusha's seven sons, including Yayati, and details Yayati's rule, his marriages, and his children.

beats.title: The Story of Yayāti and the Exchange of Ages
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the story of King Yayāti, who, cursed with old age by Śukra, asks his sons to exchange their youth for his old age. Only his youngest son Pūru agrees, and Yayāti enjoys youth for a thousand years. Eventually, he returns youth to Pūru and installs him as king, establishing the Paurava dynasty. The chapter also introduces the rivalry between the gods and demons, and their choice of priests: Aṅgiras for the gods and Uśanas (Śukra) for the demons.

beats.title: Kaca and the Mrita-sanjivani Vidya
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Kaca, son of Brihaspati, who is sent by the gods to learn the Mrita-sanjivani vidya from Shukracharya. Kaca becomes a disciple of Shukra and wins the affection of Shukra's daughter Devayani. The demons, fearing the vidya, kill Kaca and feed him to wolves, but Devayani's devotion leads to his revival.

beats.title: Kaca's Repeated Death and Revival
beats.summary: Devayani pleads with her father Shukracharya to revive Kaca, who has been killed by the Asuras. Shukracharya uses the Mrita-sanjivani vidya to call Kaca back, but Kaca is killed again and his ashes mixed with wine. Shukracharya eventually discovers Kaca inside his own stomach, teaches him the vidya, and Kaca emerges by splitting Shukracharya's belly, then revives him.

beats.title: Kaca's Departure and Devayani's Rejection
beats.summary: Kaca completes his studies with Shukra, learns the Sanjivani mantra, and is repeatedly killed by the Asuras but revived by Shukra. After being restored, he rejects Devayani's marriage proposal, citing that as his guru's daughter she is like a sister. He then departs for the celestial realm.

beats.title: Kacha's Return and the Conflict of Devayani and Sharmishtha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana narrates the story of Kacha, the son of Brihaspati, who learns the Mrita-sanjivani vidya from Shukracharya. After completing his studies, he is cursed by Devayani, Shukracharya's daughter, that the vidya will not bear fruit for him. Kacha returns to the gods, who then urge Indra to attack the demons. Meanwhile, a conflict arises between Devayani and Sharmishtha, the daughter of Vrishaparva, over a garment, leading to Sharmishtha throwing Devayani into a well. King Yayati rescues her.

beats.title: Yayāti Rescues Devayānī
beats.summary: Yayāti, son of Nahuṣa, while hunting and thirsty, finds Devayānī, daughter of Śukra, fallen into a well. He rescues her, but leaves without revealing his identity. Devayānī, feeling insulted by a previous incident with Śarmiṣṭhā, sends her maid to inform her father. Śukra arrives, consoles her, and learns of the insult. Devayānī recounts how Śarmiṣṭhā called her the daughter of one who praises and receives, while she herself is the daughter of one who is praised and gives. Śukra asserts his power and declares that he will approach Vṛṣaparvan.

beats.title: Devayani's Lesson on Anger and Shukracharya's Confrontation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapters 28-29) narrates the dialogue between Devayani and her father Shukracharya (Kavya) after she is insulted by the daughter of King Vrishaparvan. Devayani emphasizes the importance of controlling anger and enduring harsh words, stating that one who controls anger conquers all. She refuses to stay among those who do not respect her lineage and conduct. Shukracharya, angered, confronts Vrishaparvan, accusing him of allowing his daughter to harm Devayani and threatening to leave the kingdom. Vrishaparvan pleads for forgiveness, acknowledging Shukra's virtue and dharma.

beats.title: Devayani and Sharmishtha
beats.summary: King Yayati, son of Nahusha, encounters Devayani and Sharmishtha in a forest. Devayani, daughter of Shukracharya, demands that Sharmishtha become her servant. Sharmishtha agrees to avoid conflict, and Devayani reconciles with her father. Later, Devayani and Sharmishtha go to the forest to play, where Yayati sees them.

beats.title: Yayāti Meets Devayānī and Śarmiṣṭhā
beats.summary: King Yayāti encounters Devayānī and Śarmiṣṭhā in a forest. Devayānī reveals her identity as daughter of Śukra, and Śarmiṣṭhā as daughter of Vṛṣaparvan. Yayāti refuses to marry Devayānī due to caste differences, but Devayānī insists he marry her because he touched her hand. She sends a message to her father Śukra, who then confronts Yayāti.

beats.title: Yayati's Return and Sharmishtha's Approach
beats.summary: King Yayati returns to his capital with Devayani and Sharmishtha, establishing separate residences. Devayani bears a son. After a thousand years, Sharmishtha, now of age, desires a child and approaches Yayati, reminding him of her beauty and lineage. Yayati recalls Shukra's prohibition against summoning her to bed, but Sharmishtha argues that words spoken in jest or during marriage do not harm.

beats.title: Yayati's Sons and Devayani's Jealousy
beats.summary: King Yayati, married to Devayani, also has a relationship with Sharmishtha, who bears him three sons. Devayani confronts Sharmishtha, who claims the child is from a sage. Later, Devayani bears two sons, Yadu and Turvasu. Yayati and Devayani go to the forest, where Devayani sees the children playing and questions them.

beats.title: The Curse of Yayati
beats.summary: King Yayati is cursed by sage Shukracharya (Ushanas) to premature old age for violating dharma by having relations with Sharmishtha, the daughter of the demon king Vrishaparvan, while married to Devayani. The chapter narrates the confrontation between Devayani and Sharmishtha, Devayani's complaint to her father, and the curse.

beats.title: Yayāti's Curse and the Refusal of Sons
beats.summary: King Yayāti, cursed by Śukra (Uśanas) to premature old age, asks his sons to exchange their youth for his old age. Yadu and Turvasu refuse, earning curses; Druhyu also refuses. The story continues with Yayāti's curse and the sons' fates.

beats.title: Yayāti's Exchange of Old Age for Youth
beats.summary: King Yayāti, cursed with old age by Śukra, asks his sons to exchange their youth for his old age. Only his youngest son Pūru agrees. Yayāti enjoys youth for a thousand years, then realizes that desire is never satisfied by indulgence, and returns the youth to Pūru, taking back his old age.

beats.title: Yayāti's Exchange of Old Age and the Dynasties
beats.summary: King Yayāti, after enjoying youth, transfers his old age to his youngest son Pūru, who accepts it. Yayāti then crowns Pūru as king and retires to the forest. The story explains the origin of the Yādava, Yavana, Bhoja, Mleccha, and Pauravā dynasties.

beats.title: The Story of King Yayāti: Renunciation and the Virtue of Forbearance
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Yayāti, who after ruling righteously, renounces his kingdom in favor of his youngest son Pūru and retires to the forest to perform severe austerities. He eventually attains heaven, but later, during a conversation with Indra, he explains the virtues of forbearance and non-anger, emphasizing that a wise person endures insults without retaliation, thereby burning the sinner's merit.

beats.title: The Fall of Yayāti
beats.summary: Yayāti, son of Nahuṣa, falls from heaven due to pride and disrespect. He is received by King Aṣṭaka and other sages, who teach him the value of humility and respect for the wise. The chapter emphasizes the importance of gentle speech, honoring the virtuous, and the transience of heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Yayāti's Discourse on Fate and the Fall from Heaven
beats.summary: King Yayāti, after falling from heaven, explains to his grandson Aṣṭaka the nature of fate (diṣṭa) and the impermanence of pleasure and pain. He describes his celestial experiences and the inevitability of falling when merit is exhausted, emphasizing equanimity and acceptance of destiny.

beats.title: Discourse on Rebirth and Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapters 39-40) discusses the journey of the soul after death, the process of rebirth, the nature of karma, and the means to attain liberation. It emphasizes the importance of tapas (austerity), dana (charity), and other virtues, while warning against pride and hypocrisy.

beats.title: The Ascetic's Path and the Falling King
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana discusses the characteristics and practices of different types of ascetics (munis), including those who live in forests and those who live in villages, and the spiritual attainments possible through disciplined life. It also includes a dialogue between a king and a falling being about the nature of existence and the results of actions.

beats.title: Yayāti's Fall and the Dialogue on Merit
beats.summary: King Yayāti, falling from heaven, asks King Vasumān (Auṣadaśvi) about the existence of heavenly worlds earned by his merits. Vasumān offers his own worlds to Yayāti, but Yayāti refuses to accept what he has not earned himself. The dialogue continues with King Śibi (Auśīnara) also offering worlds, but Yayāti insists on earning his own merits. Ultimately, celestial chariots appear for the righteous kings.

beats.title: Yayāti's Ascent and Yadu's Genealogy
beats.summary: King Yayāti, son of Nahuṣa, recounts his life and deeds to his grandsons Aṣṭaka, Pratardana, Vasumān, and Śibi. He describes his conquest of the earth, his gifts to Brahmins, and his adherence to truth. The grandsons help him ascend to heaven. Later, King Śatānīka hears this story from the sage Śaunaka and honors him. The chapter then begins the genealogy of Yadu's descendants.

beats.title: The Lineage and Glory of Kārtavīrya Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the lineage of the Haihaya kings, focusing on the mighty king Kārtavīrya Arjuna (also known as Sahasrabāhu). It details his birth, his thousand arms, his conquest of the seven continents, his performance of numerous sacrifices, and his extraordinary powers, including controlling the ocean and subduing the Nāga Karkoṭaka.

beats.title: The Story of Kārtavīrya Arjuna and the Burning of the Hermitage
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana describes the story of King Kārtavīrya Arjuna, a thousand-armed king who subdued the ocean and defeated Rāvaṇa. It recounts his conflict with the sage Āpava (Paraśurāma), who cursed him after Kārtavīrya burned his hermitage. The chapter also lists the lineages of the Haihaya kings, including Kārtavīrya's sons and descendants, and concludes with a question about why the forest was burned.

beats.title: The Curse of Āpava and the Yādava Lineage
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Kārtavīrya Arjuna, who receives divine arrows from the Sun god (Āditya) and uses them to burn forests, including the hermitage of the sage Āpava. The sage curses him, leading to the lineage of the Yādavas through Kroṣṭu, culminating in Viṣṇu's incarnation in the Vṛṣṇi clan.

beats.title: The Yadava Genealogy: From Rukmakavaca to the Sātvata Clan
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapter 44) traces the lineage of the Yadava dynasty, starting from King Rukmakavaca and his sons, through Jyāmagha and his descendants, including Vidarbha, Kratha, Kaiśika, and others, leading to the Sātvata clan and the birth of prominent figures like Bhajamāna, Andhaka, Vṛṣṇi, and others.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Yadava Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapter 44, verses 48-71) narrates the genealogy of the Yadava dynasty, focusing on the descendants of Bhajamana through his wives Srnjayya and Bahyaka. It details the births of kings such as Babhru, Devavrdha, and the line of Kukura, Vrishni, and Ahuka, leading to the births of Devaka and Ugrasena, ancestors of Krishna.

beats.title: Yadava Genealogy and the Syamantaka Jewel
beats.summary: This chapter from the Matsya Purana (Chapters 44-45) details the genealogy of the Yadava dynasty, focusing on the descendants of Devaka and Ugrasena, the lineage of Andhakas, and the story of the Syamantaka jewel involving Prasena and Govinda (Krishna).
